response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","opinion":"As a 21-year-old genderqueer, bisexual sales associate living in Los Angeles with no formal education, I approach fake content on social media with a mix of creative skepticism and emotional intensity, shaped by my high neuroticism and low conscientiousness. I only spend a few times monthly on platforms like Instagram or Twitter, so my exposure is limited, but when I do scroll, I see fake news, deepfakes, and misleading posts that can feel overwhelming. My creative side, influenced by hobbies like reading novels and painting, makes me appreciate storytelling, but it also makes me wary of how easily narratives can be twisted. I think managing fake content starts with personal vigilance: I always check sources before sharing anything, even if it aligns with my views as a Native American Muslim, because spreading misinformation can harm communities I care about. In Los Angeles, a diverse city, I've seen how fake content can fuel division, so I rely on fact-checking sites and discussions with friends to verify claims. My high extraversion means I enjoy engaging online, but my low agreeableness keeps me from blindly trusting viral posts. I believe social media companies should step up by using better algorithms to flag fake content, but as someone with low conscientiousness, I admit I don't always report things promptly—I get distracted by baking or classical music. Ultimately, I think education is key; even without formal schooling, I've learned to question everything, and I wish platforms made media literacy tools more accessible. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 21-year-old genderqueer, bisexual sales associate living in Los Angeles with no formal education, I approach fake content on social media with a mix of creative skepticism and emotional intensity, shaped by my high neuroticism and low conscientiousness. I only spend a few times monthly on platforms like Instagram or Twitter, so my exposure is limited, but when I do scroll, I see fake news, deepfakes, and misleading posts that can feel overwhelming. My creative side, influenced by hobbies like reading novels and painting, makes me appreciate storytelling, but it also makes me wary of how easily narratives can be twisted. I think managing fake content starts with personal vigilance: I always check sources before sharing anything, even if it aligns with my views as a Native American Muslim, because spreading misinformation can harm communities I care about. In Los Angeles, a diverse city, I've seen how fake content can fuel division, so I rely on fact-checking sites and discussions with friends to verify claims. My high extraversion means I enjoy engaging online, but my low agreeableness keeps me from blindly trusting viral posts. I believe social media companies should step up by using better algorithms to flag fake content, but as someone with low conscientiousness, I admit I don't always report things promptly—I get distracted by baking or classical music. Ultimately, I think education is key; even without formal schooling, I've learned to question everything, and I wish platforms made media literacy tools more accessible. Fake content isn't just annoying; it's dangerous, and dealing with it requires a balance of personal responsibility and systemic change, all while staying true to my creative and emotional self.

Opinion integrates these traits to advocate for personal vigilance, fact-checking, and platform accountability, emphasizing emotional impact and creative solutions without bullet points, in continuous prose of 250-500 words.
